chunk_summary: This section discusses the indications and thresholds for fortifying expressed, donor, and banked milk, particularly for preterm and low-birth-weight infants, based on updated guidelines and recommendations from authoritative sources.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for fortifying expressed and donor milk in preterm infants.
Identify the nutritional thresholds that necessitate milk fortification.
Review evidence-based guidelines for milk fortification from global health organizations.
Explore the role of milk banks in providing fortified milk for vulnerable populations.
key_facts: Milk fortification is recommended for preterm infants to meet their higher nutritional needs.
The European Milk Bank Association emphasizes fortification for infants weighing less than 1500 grams.
WHO guidelines highlight the importance of fortification for low-birth-weight infants (<2500 grams).
Fortified milk should provide adequate protein, calcium, and phosphorus to support growth and bone development.
Donor milk is often fortified to match the nutritional profile of maternal milk for preterm infants.
Nutritional needs of preterm infants are significantly higher than those of term infants, requiring tailored interventions.
reference_quote: Fortification of human milk for preterm infants: update and recommendations of the European Milk Bank Association Working Group on human milk fortification — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6439523/
document_key_topics: Role of donor milk and milk banks in neonatal care
Guidelines from WHO and European Milk Bank Association
